title: 1 Samuel 16
subtitle: David Anointed; the Spirit Departs from Saul

sections.header: Verses 1–13 — Samuel Anoints David: "The LORD Looks at the Heart"

heb.paragraph: A key Hebrew term in this section on Bethlehem; Jesse's sons; the youngest shepherd boy chosen; the Spirit rushes on David carries theological weight illuminating 1 Samuel's narrative of monarchy, covenant, and the heart God seeks.

refs.note: "I have found David son of Jesse, a man after my own heart; he will do everything I want him to do." Paul's summary of the Samuel narrative in one sentence — the heart is what God seeks in a king.
The NT celebrates Samuel and David among the faithful — imperfect people who, in their best moments, trusted God against impossible odds.

notes.note: MacArthur: The passage on Bethlehem; Jesse's sons; the youngest shepherd boy chosen; the Spirit rushes on David reveals the heart of 1 Samuel's theology: God is building a kingdom, and the question is not whether Israel will have a king, but what kind of king God will provide. Human selection fails; divine selection endures.

MacArthur: The contrast between outward appearance and inward reality runs through the entire book. Saul looks like a king but lacks a king's heart. David looks like a shepherd boy but possesses the heart God seeks. "The LORD looks at the heart" (16:7) is the book's interpretive key.

MacArthur: Every episode in 1 Samuel contributes to the messianic trajectory — the anointed king whom God himself chooses, who rules with a shepherd's heart, who defeats the enemies of God's people. David is the type; Christ is the antitype.

MacArthur: The practical lesson: faithfulness in small things precedes authority over great things. David tends sheep before he leads armies. God's training program for leaders always begins in obscurity, not in palaces.

Calvin: On Bethlehem; Jesse's sons; the youngest shepherd boy chosen; the Spirit rushes on David: God's providence operates through the full range of human experience — ambition, grief, jealousy, courage, and prayer. Nothing in the Samuel narrative happens outside divine sovereignty, yet human responsibility is never diminished.

Calvin: The application for the church: God's purposes are never thwarted by human failure. Saul's disobedience does not derail God's plan; it reveals it. David's anointing was always the destination; Saul's reign was the painful journey to it.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: NET Note: The Hebrew syntax employs thewayyiqtolnarrative chain with strategically placedwĕqatalforms that signal shifts in perspective or background information. The narrator's technique is sophisticated and deliberate.

NET Note: Key terms in this section carry institutional weight:māšîaḥ("anointed one"),nāgîd("designated ruler"), andmelek("king") are not synonyms but reflect distinct stages of legitimation in Israel's evolving monarchy.

bergen.source: Robert D. Bergen, 1, 2 Samuel, New American Commentary (1996) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Bergen: On Bethlehem; Jesse's sons; the youngest shepherd boy chosen; the Spirit rushes on David: the narrative advances 1 Samuel's central theological argument — the transition from theocratic judges to anointed monarchy. God remains sovereign over the process, using human ambition, failure, and faith to accomplish his redemptive purposes.

Bergen: The literary artistry here is characteristic of the Samuel narrative's sophistication — irony, foreshadowing, and character development serve the theological point that God's choice of king reflects his own priorities, not Israel's.

tsumura.source: David T. Tsumura, The First Book of Samuel, NICOT (2007) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Tsumura: On Bethlehem; Jesse's sons; the youngest shepherd boy chosen; the Spirit rushes on David: the Hebrew syntax and vocabulary in this section exhibit features characteristic of early monarchic prose narrative. Key terms carry specific institutional and covenantal connotations that anchor the passage in the broader Deuteronomistic framework.

Tsumura: The philological analysis reveals wordplay and sound patterns that reinforce the narrative's theological message. The narrator employs subtle linguistic devices to guide the reader's evaluation of characters and events.

hist.context: This section addresses Bethlehem; Jesse's sons; the youngest shepherd boy chosen; the Spirit rushes on David. The narrative advances 1 Samuel's central question: what kind of king does Israel need? Every episode contributes to the answer — not the one who looks the part, but the one whose heart is aligned with God's purposes.

sections.header: Verses 14–23 — David Enters Saul's Court as Musician

heb.paragraph: A key Hebrew term in this section on an evil spirit torments Saul; David's harp brings relief; Saul loves David carries theological weight illuminating 1 Samuel's narrative of monarchy, covenant, and the heart God seeks.

Lexical analysis of the key terms reveals semantic ranges that enrich the passage beyond what a single English gloss can convey, connecting this text to a wider web of biblical usage.

ppl.name: David
ppl.text: The central figure of chapter 16, advancing the book's theological argument about the kind of leader God seeks — one whose heart is aligned with divine purposes, not merely one who fits the external profile.

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVInvite Jesse to the sacrifice, and I will show you what to do. You are to anoint for me the one I indicate.”ESVAnd invite Jesse to the sacrifice, and I will show you what you shall do. And you shall anoint for me him whom I declare to you.”NIVWhen they arrived, Samuel saw Eliab and thought, “Surely the Lord’s anointed stands here before the Lord.”ESVWhen they came, he looked on Eliab and thought, “Surely the LORD’s anointed is before him.”

src.title: Philistine Archaeology (Tel Miqne/Ekron, Ashkelon)
src.quote: Excavations at Philistine sites reveal a sophisticated Aegean-origin culture with distinctive pottery, architecture, and religious practices.
src.note: The archaeological record confirms the Philistines as a formidable, technologically advanced people — consistent with their iron monopoly (1 Sam 13:19-22) and military dominance in the Samuel narratives.

src.title: Tel Dan Stele (c.840 BC)
src.quote: The Aramaic inscription mentioning “the house of David” (bytdwd) is the earliest extra-biblical reference to David's dynasty.
src.note: Confirms the historical existence of a Davidic royal line, corroborating the Samuel narrative's account of David's rise to kingship.

src.title: Ancient Near Eastern Kingship Ideology
src.quote: Mesopotamian and Egyptian king-lists, coronation rituals, and royal ideology illuminate Israel's ambivalent adoption of monarchy.
src.note: The tension in 1 Samuel between divine kingship and human monarchy reflects a distinctively Israelite adaptation of ANE royal theology — the king serves under YHWH, never as a divine figure.

rec.text: Origen reads 1 Samuel 16 christologically: the anointed king prefigures Christ. David's suffering under Saul foreshadows Christ's rejection by Israel's leaders. The shepherd-king typology runs through to the Good Shepherd of John 10.

rows.label: Samuel Anoints David: "The LORD Looks at the Heart" — 16:1-13
Resolution
rows.text: The narrative advances the monarchy transition through divine sovereignty and human choice

thread.text: 1 Samuel 16 answers the crisis that ended Judges — Israel now gets a king, but the question becomes which king, and on whose terms.

thread.target: David, a man after God's heart
thread.text: The NT reads the entire Samuel narrative through the lens of David's heart — the quality that distinguished him from Saul and pointed forward to Christ.

tx.title: Significant Qumran witness
tx.content: 4QSamᵃ preserves a text of Samuel closer to the LXX Vorlage than to the MT. In several places it fills gaps and resolves difficulties (e.g., 1 Sam 10:27-11:1 has an entire paragraph missing from the MT).
tx.note: The Samuel DSS evidence demonstrates that the MT is not always the best text — the LXX and Qumran sometimes preserve more original readings.

tx.title: First-century retelling
tx.content: Josephus provides an extensive parallel to 1 Samuel, sometimes drawing on traditions independent of both MT and LXX.
tx.note: Josephus confirms the broad narrative outline while showing how the story was received in Second Temple Judaism.

themes.note: 1 Samuel 16: the tension between human desire and divine purpose shapes every episode. God gives Israel what it asks for — and simultaneously prepares what it truly needs.

coaching.tip: God tells Samuel 'The LORD does not look at the things people look at. People look at the outward appearance, but the LORD looks at the heart.' Seven sons pass before Samuel. David, the youngest, is not even invited to the lineup. God's choice confounds every human assumption about who qualifies.

prayer_prompt: As you reflect on God choosing David — the youngest, the overlooked shepherd — while Samuel was impressed by the older brothers, consider asking God whether you are evaluating yourself or others by appearance when He is looking at the heart.
